name: Idris 2
summary: Edwin Brady's dependently-typed language whose distinguishing contribution is Quantitative Type Theory: multiplicities (0, 1, ω) attached to every binder, integrating linearity, runtime erasure, and proof-vs-execution separation in one type-theoretic dimension. Effects library and dependent effects make protocol checking expressible in the type system.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Idris and Idris 2 are especially valuable when the boundary between type systems and effects becomes blurry. The Idris Effects library and later Idris 2's Quantitative Type Theory show how a language can track not only what type a computation returns, but also which resources, protocol states, or capabilities are available before and after an effectful action.

sections.title: Dependent effects — protocol-checking in the type system
sections.content: Dependent effects let the availability or shape of an effect depend on runtime outcomes that are then discharged by pattern matching. This is especially powerful for protocol checking: opening a file can yield a different postcondition depending on success, and the type checker can force the program to account for both outcomes.

sources.label: Brady — Programming and reasoning with algebraic effects and dependent types

sections.title: Quantitative Type Theory — multiplicities in the core
sections.content:
Idris 2's **Quantitative Type Theory** adds multiplicities to binders, bringing linearity and erasure directly into the core type theory. The practical payoff is that type-level reasoning about resource use and protocol state can be expressed in one language rather than split across a type system and a separate effect discipline.

Every binder carries a multiplicity from {0, 1, ω}: 0-quantity values are erased before runtime; 1-quantity values must be used exactly once; ω-quantity values are unrestricted. Both type-level data and erased proof witnesses ride as 0; runtime values are 1 or ω. The IR consequence is owned by `data/idris2-qtt-ir`.

QTT's design contribution is that one multiplicity dimension subsumes both Granule's grades (`data/granule`) and traditional erasure markers, keeping the IR simpler than carrying them as separate annotations. Compared to ATS (`data/ats-language`), QTT integrates linearity and dependent typing without the separate statics/dynamics strata, with cleaner ergonomics at the cost of a richer kernel.
